_comment: One benchmark run = one of these. make-report.py renders it into the on-brand report. Presentation-free where possible; the generator derives colors/styles from role/kind. Copy follows NOL8 brand voice: no em dashes, no exclamation marks, no emoji; Aergia is always the RE2 benchmark, never a NOL8 product.
lede: Govern known-value risk before it reaches the vector index. Themis runs the policy inline on FPGA. To prove it, we ran the identical policy and corpus through Aergia, our stand-up of Google RE2, as the benchmark. Same data. Same policy. Byte-identical output. Compute cost is sub-millisecond, so nearly all observed latency is the network path.

stats.label: of per-call latency is network + TLS

benchmark.heading: One policy. Two engines. Identical output.
benchmark.lede: The same literal known-value policy runs the same 1,000-chunk corpus on both engines: Themis on FPGA, and Aergia, our stand-up of Google RE2, as the benchmark. Do nothing is the null baseline.

approaches.headline: Everything reaches the index
approaches.desc: All 43,005 tokens forwarded downstream, sensitive values included.

approaches.headline: Google RE2, the known regex engine
approaches.desc: Our sandbox stand-up of Google RE2, running the identical policy on CPU. The established software baseline we measure against.

approaches.headline: Deterministic known-value governance
approaches.desc: The identical policy, inline on FPGA at hardware speed. Byte-identical output to the RE2 benchmark, and sub-millisecond per call.

redaction.before: Customer review: Westbridge Merchant Services requested accelerated enablement for the Redwood Identity workflow.
redaction.note: Byte-identical across Themis and Aergia for all 1,000 records

latency.heading: The engine is the smallest slice.
latency.lede: We isolated raw engine time by measuring the path in layers and subtracting transport. N = 100, medians, from the client box. On both engines the compute lands below the network round-trip, so it is reported as an upper bound.
latency.callout: Reusing one connection instead of reconnecting per call drops Themis from 7.17 ms to 2.38 ms, a 3x cut with zero engine change. What is left is one network round-trip. The engine sits inside the measurement noise.

tableNote: Engine time is what remains after subtracting network RTT and TLS. On both engines it lands under the network round-trip, below what we can resolve from outside, so it is reported as an upper bound. The takeaway is not a precise figure. It is that the engine is not where the time goes.

meaning.heading: What it means for the data path.

takeaways.title: Governance is free at the compute layer.
takeaways.body: Sub-millisecond per call means deterministic control sits inline on the data path with no latency-budget conversation. Themis keeps up with the wire.

takeaways.title: Your latency is a networking problem, not a compute one.
takeaways.body: About 97% of observed per-call time is TLS setup and round-trip. Shorter, warmer, more direct paths translate almost one-to-one into end-to-end improvement.

takeaways.title: The first win is connection reuse.
takeaways.body: Pooling connections alone is a 3x per-call improvement here, before touching anything else.

takeaways.title: Parity with the RE2 benchmark, at hardware speed.
takeaways.body: Themis matches Aergia byte-for-byte on the identical policy and corpus. NOL8 is provably as correct as the established regex engine, and it runs inline on FPGA where legacy software middleware cannot.

method.def: 1,000-chunk synthetic pre-index corpus, run identically through every approach.
One literal known-value policy: denied entities, watched customers, compromised accounts, payment cards, internal projects, blocked IPs.
Themis, the NOL8 FPGA (:443), and Aergia, our stand-up of Google RE2 (:444) as the benchmark. Same data, same policy, byte-identical output. Do nothing forwards everything.
Exact. Chunks governed = chunks containing a governed value.
Decomposed from the field client box, N = 100 per layer, medians.
Bounded as (warm minus network RTT). Lands within network jitter, so it is reported as an upper bound. No server-side timing hook is exposed.
Cold = new TLS connection per request (current adapter). Warm = pooled.

methodNote: Not a concurrency or throughput saturation test.

raw.heading: Full benchmark data
raw.note: Per-approach measurements from the 1,000-chunk run, harness client process. Throughput (chunks/sec) is end-to-end and includes the network round-trip for the engines, so it is not an engine rate. CPU below the timer's 0.01s resolution is shown as <0.01: Do nothing completes in about 10 ms, so its CPU rounds under the floor (cpu_percent was 100% for that window). Max RSS is the working set; Do nothing loads no reference lists, HTTP client, or regex, so it is the smallest.

footer.tagline: The AI data plane. Hardware-class, inline, wire-speed. Governance runs free at the compute layer.
